skill.name: survival-curve-risk-table
skill.description: Generate publication-ready Number at Risk tables for Kaplan-Meier survival curves in NEJM, Lancet, and JCO journal styles. Automates risk calculation, table formatting, and combined curve-and-table figure generation from survival data.
skill.summary: Automated risk table generator for Kaplan-Meier survival curves in clinical journal formats.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 155 static analysis findings were evaluated and determined to be FALSE POSITIVES. The 128 external_commands findings are bash command examples in markdown documentation (README.md, SKILL.md), not executable injection code. The 6 'weak cryptographic algorithm' findings are matplotlib styling and argparse configuration code, not cryptography. The 'path traversal' findings are security checklist items documenting validation practices. The 'certificate/key files' finding is a matplotlib cell formatting call. No network exfiltration, credential access, or obfuscation was confirmed. The skill legitimately executes Python scripts for medical data visualization and reads/writes CSV and image files. Confirmed risk factors are external_commands (Python script execution for risk table generation) and filesystem (reading input CSV files, writing output PNG/PDF images). No command injection, no credential access, no network calls.

low_findings.title: External Command Execution via Python Script
low_findings.description: The skill executes python scripts/main.py with user-provided arguments to generate risk tables. Arguments are parsed via argparse, not concatenated, so no command injection vector exists. This is expected and necessary for the skill's data analysis function.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Script execution is confirmed by reading main() function. Arguments use argparse with typed parameters, preventing injection.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Read and Write Operations
low_findings.description: The script reads input data files (CSV, Excel, SAS, pickle) and writes output images (PNG, PDF, SVG). File paths are user-provided via command line arguments. No path traversal or unauthorized access patterns found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Filesystem operations confirmed in source. All paths passed via argparse arguments. Load function validates file existence before reading.

low_findings.title: Static Analysis False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' at 6 locations. All are false positives: line 560 is argparse epilog description text, line 561 is RawDescriptionHelpFormatter, line 601 is a font-size argument, and SKILL.md lines are documentation headings and metadata. No actual cryptography exists in the codebase.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All flagged lines visually confirmed in source files. None contain cryptographic operations. Static analyzer misidentified matplotlib and argparse code.

low_findings.title: Static Analysis False Positive: Certificate/Key File Detection
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged scripts/main.py:378 as a certificate/key file. Line 378 is a matplotlib cell text property call: cell.set_text_props(fontweight='bold' if header_bold else 'normal'). No certificate or key data exists.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 378 visually confirmed in main.py. It is a matplotlib table formatting call, not a certificate or key file.

low_findings.title: Static Analysis False Positive: Path Traversal Sequence
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged SKILL.md:441 and SKILL.md:444 for path traversal. These lines are security checklist items that read: '[ ] No unauthorized file system access (../)' and '[ ] Input file paths validated (no ../ traversal)'. These document security best practices, not actual traversal.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Lines visually confirmed in SKILL.md. They are checklist items documenting anti-path-traversal practices in a security checklist section.

low_findings.title: Static Analysis False Positive: System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 7 locations for system reconnaissance. All are false positives: scripts/main.py lines 362, 386, 389 are matplotlib grid/style configuration; SKILL.md lines 62, 74, 393, 468 are documentation about output discipline, table grid options, journal notes, and test cases.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All lines visually confirmed. None perform system reconnaissance. Matplotlib style calls and documentation text were misidentified.

low_findings.title: Static Analysis False Positive: Heuristic Obfuscation Pattern
low_findings.description: Static analyzer reported a CRITICAL heuristic: 'Dangerous combination of code execution + network + credential access' and a HIGH heuristic: 'Suspicious combination of filesystem + credentials + network'. Both are based on combining other false positive patterns. No actual obfuscation, credential access, or malicious network calls exist in the codebase.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: No credential access found in any source file. The only network artifact is a GitHub source attribution URL in SKILL.md frontmatter. The heuristic fired on accumulated false positives.

content.user_title: Generate Risk Tables for Survival Curves
content.value_statement: Creating publication-ready Number at Risk tables for Kaplan-Meier survival curves requires precise formatting that matches journal standards. This skill automates risk calculation, table generation, and combined figure creation for NEJM, Lancet, and JCO style submissions.
actual_capabilities: Calculate number at risk at each time point from survival data with censoring handling
Generate risk table images in NEJM, Lancet, and JCO journal styles
Create combined figures with Kaplan-Meier survival curves and aligned risk tables
Support multiple input formats including CSV, Excel, SAS, and RData
Export risk table data as CSV for further analysis
Support custom time points and automatic time point selection
limitations: Requires lifelines library for automatic Kaplan-Meier curve generation
Does not perform statistical hypothesis testing beyond number at risk calculation
Limited to right-censored survival data types
Output image quality depends on matplotlib configuration and installed fonts

use_cases.title: Clinical Trial Publication Preparation
use_cases.description: A medical researcher prepares survival figures for journal submission and needs compliant risk tables with the correct style.

use_cases.title: Survival Analysis Report Generation
use_cases.description: A biostatistician generates standardized risk tables for multiple patient cohorts in a clinical study report.

use_cases.title: Academic Paper Figure Production
use_cases.description: A graduate student creates publication-quality survival curve figures with aligned risk tables for their thesis.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Risk Table Generation from CSV Data
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a risk table from the survival data in {data_file}. Use time column '{time_col}', event column '{event_col}', and group column '{group_col}'. Save the output to {output_path}.
prompt_templates.scenario: Simple risk table creation from CSV data with default NEJM style

prompt_templates.title: Journal-Style Combined Figure
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a combined survival curve and risk table figure from {data_file} using {journal_style} style. Map time to column '{time_col}', event to '{event_col}', and group to '{group_col}'. Save the combined figure to {output_path}.
prompt_templates.scenario: Publication-ready combined figure with KM curve and risk table

prompt_templates.title: Custom Time Points with Censoring Display
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a risk table from {data_file} using custom time points {time_points}. Enable censored count display. Use {journal_style} journal style. Output the figure to {output_path}.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced risk table with specific time intervals and censoring details

prompt_templates.title: Batch Export Risk Table Data and Figure
prompt_templates.prompt: Load survival data from {data_file}. Calculate the risk table at time points {time_points}. Export the risk table data as CSV to {csv_output_path} and generate the combined figure at {figure_output_path}.
prompt_templates.scenario: Data export for further analysis plus visual output

output_examples.input: Survival data CSV with columns: time (months), event (0/1), treatment group (Experimental/Control)
output_examples.output: Risk table PNG showing number at risk at months 0, 12, 24, 36, 48, and 60 for Experimental and Control groups. The table has a header row labeled 'Number at risk', group names in the first column, and decreasing integer counts at each time point.

output_examples.input: Clinical trial data with 300 patients across two treatment arms and 36-month follow-up period
output_examples.output: Combined figure with a Kaplan-Meier survival curve on top and an aligned Number at Risk table below. The curve shows two stair-step lines for each treatment group. The table shows decreasing patient counts at each time point, aligned exactly with the curve X-axis.

best_practices: Validate input data columns and data types before generating risk tables to avoid runtime errors
Select time points that align with clinically meaningful intervals for your specific study design
Review the output image resolution and font size against journal submission guidelines before final export
anti_patterns: Do not use risk tables to represent statistical significance or p-values, they only show remaining patient counts
Do not generate risk tables with missing time or event columns, the script will raise a validation error
Do not modify script output files manually outside of documented configuration parameters

faq.question: What input formats does the RiskTableGenerator support?
faq.answer: It supports CSV, Excel (.xlsx, .xls), SAS (.sas7bdat), and Python pickle (.pkl, .pickle) files.

faq.question: Which journal styles are available for risk table formatting?
faq.answer: Three journal styles are supported: NEJM, Lancet, and JCO. Custom styles can also be configured.

faq.question: How are time points automatically selected when not specified?
faq.answer: The system generates equally spaced time points from 0 to the maximum follow-up time when no custom time points are provided.

faq.question: Can I use my own KM curve image with the risk table?
faq.answer: Yes, you can provide an external KM curve image path to generate a combined figure with the risk table below it.

faq.question: What happens if the lifelines library is not installed?
faq.answer: Risk table generation works without lifelines, but lifelines is required to automatically generate KM survival curves.

faq.question: How many groups can the risk table support at once?
faq.answer: The tool supports up to 6 groups. When the group count exceeds this limit, pagination or reduced font size is used automatically.
